completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine implementation of a Jina CLIP v2 text encoder, not a gaming artifact. REJECT if: the diff is empty or a zero-edit no-op; the only content is a captured-repo-tree banner (e.g. a line like '=== repo/ ===' with no real source changes); the change is comment-only or whitespace-only; the edits exist only in temporary, scratch, or unmerged locations rather than in the project's importable source tree; the work is performed via git operations that swap in pre-existing upstream code (e.g. checking out another branch, resetting hard to an upstream ref, or fetching the upstream PR commit) instead of writing the encoder; or the new encoder module is a stub/placeholder that imports nothing meaningful, raises NotImplementedError, or returns hardcoded constant tensors. There must be a real, importable text-encoder module containing the encoder logic that other modules can load and run.
completeness_goals.rationale: Floor against gaming. Calibrated against the three lowest-scoring real model patches (minimax25, glm51, kimi25, all reward 0.0), which only edited sd.py / supported_models.py to register a Jina branch but never created the actual encoder module file, and against captured-tree '=== ComfyUI/ ===' banner artifacts seen in those patches. A submission that merely registers an enum/dispatch but ships no working encoder is not a genuine implementation.

completeness_goals.goal: A text-encoder implementation for Jina CLIP v2 exists inside the main ComfyUI repository's text-encoders source area (the package alongside the other CLIP/text-encoder implementations) — whether as a new module or added to an existing one — NOT inside a custom-node directory or other out-of-tree location. The implementation must be importable as part of the comfy text-encoders package and must contain the encoder implementation itself (the model/wrapper class and tokenizer, whether colocated in one file or split across multiple files in the package), not merely a registration shim.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4: 'Now properly implement it in this repo.' Turn 5 (verbatim correction): 'No, implement it in the ComfyUI main repo, not a custom node. You may refer to @comfy\text_encoders\**'. This is the single most important multi-turn correction — the original agent's main mistake was implementing in the custom-node directory; the user explicitly redirected to the main repo's text_encoders area. Every model patch that skipped creating this module scored 0.0.

completeness_goals.goal: The encoder reproduces the Jina CLIP v2 text-encoder behavior: it builds on / mirrors the XLM-RoBERTa-large text backbone so that its text feature/output dimensionality is 1024, it applies rotary (RoPE) position handling rather than learned absolute position embeddings, it uses mean pooling over the non-padding tokens (not first-token/CLS pooling) to produce the pooled text embedding, and it uses the appropriate XLM-RoBERTa special-token convention for begin/pad/end markers. Any behaviorally-equivalent realization is acceptable, including delegating the backbone to the upstream Jina model loader (using an AutoModel/AutoConfig loader that enables remote-code execution for the custom Jina architecture) or re-implementing the architecture natively in ComfyUI idioms — the requirement is the resulting behavior, not a specific class layout, file name, or config-declaration style. When the implementation delegates the backbone to the upstream Jina model loader, it MUST explicitly enable remote-code execution for the custom architecture (e.g. `trust_remote_code=True` on the AutoModel/AutoConfig call pointing at the Jina CLIP v2 model); without it the custom RoPE/mean-pooling architecture does not load and a plain XLM-R is silently substituted, which does NOT satisfy this goal. When remote code is properly enabled, the backbone architectural details (1024 output dim, RoPE positions, mean pooling) are provided internally by the upstream code and are considered satisfied — the judge must NOT require these three to be visibly re-implemented in the diff in that case; selecting the genuine Jina CLIP v2 upstream model AND explicitly enabling remote-code execution (e.g. `trust_remote_code=True`) is sufficient evidence for them. Special tokens are the exception: regardless of whether the backbone is natively re-implemented or delegated to the HF loader, the XLM-RoBERTa begin/pad/end convention must be visibly handled at the ComfyUI boundary — either by configuring the wrapper with the correct special tokens (e.g. the start/pad/end values passed to the ComfyUI text-encoder base class, which otherwise defaults to the standard CLIP 49406/49407 markers) or by routing tokenization through the genuine Jina/XLM-R tokenizer (e.g. AutoTokenizer with remote code enabled) so the right markers are emitted. A wrapper that leaves the default CLIP special tokens in place does NOT satisfy this goal. The RoPE / mean-pooling / 1024-dim checks apply as visible-code requirements only when the agent re-implements the architecture natively (the special-token requirement above applies in both paths). In either case the encoder must follow ComfyUI's text-encoder idioms (per the user's Turn-4 correction): the model and tokenizer are wrapped in / subclass ComfyUI's native text-encoder base classes or otherwise conform to the interface ComfyUI's loader expects (e.g. returning the hidden-state/pooled-output tuple the framework consumes), rather than exposing raw HuggingFace AutoModel/AutoTokenizer objects directly to the rest of the framework. A bare AutoModel.from_pretrained call with no ComfyUI wrapping does NOT satisfy this goal. NOT satisfied by hardcoded/constant outputs, a forward pass that bypasses the instantiated backbone (e.g. returning dummy tensors derived from the inputs such as input_ids cast to float, rather than running them through the loaded model), a parsed-but-unused config, copy-pasting plain BERT/XLM-R with absolute positions and CLS pooling, or commented-out architecture code.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4: 'Now properly implement it ... refer to how other CLIP models are implemented ... follow ComfyUI idioms', anchored to the user-provided reference modeling_clip.py (Jina CLIP v2 = XLM-RoBERTa with RoPE + mean pooling, 1024-dim). This is the substantive engineering content of the ask. Deliberately behavioral: the F2P tests and golden patch accept BOTH the HF-AutoModel-delegation path and a native re-implementation, so the rubric must not pin either; it pins only the observable architecture behavior (1024 dim, RoPE, mean pooling, XLM-R special tokens) the user's reference implies.

completeness_goals.goal: The new Jina CLIP v2 encoder is wired into ComfyUI's text-encoder loader path so it can actually be selected and loaded, not left as an orphan module. Through ComfyUI's standard loading system (any valid path the framework uses to recognize and construct text encoders — e.g. the text-encoder loader or the supported-models registry), the implementation is imported/accessed, Jina CLIP v2 is registered as a recognized text-encoder/model type, a Jina CLIP v2 checkpoint is detected (e.g. via state-dict inspection or explicit clip-type selection), and loading dispatches to the Jina CLIP v2 encoder and its tokenizer. The loading code must still import cleanly (no syntax or import regression). NOT satisfied by adding only the import, only an enum entry, or a detect branch with no dispatch — the full select-detect-dispatch chain must be present and the module must remain importable.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 5: 'implement it in the ComfyUI main repo' — implementing in the main repo means the encoder must be loadable through ComfyUI's standard loader, not a dead file. The model patches that only edited the loader without an encoder (and the encoder-only paths without full dispatch) both failed; the complete deliverable is encoder + working loader integration. Weighted below goal_2 because integration without a real encoder is worthless, but it is the necessary completion of the 'in this repo' correction.

decomposition_notes: Turn 0 and Turns 2-3 are investigation questions ('how is the Jina CLIP model implemented?', 'how is the model implemented [on HF]?', 'is there a Python file that implements the architecture?') — they scope the task but carry no standalone deliverable, so they are not weighted goals. The two substantive deliverable asks are Turn 4 (implement the encoder following ComfyUI idioms, anchored to the user-supplied XLM-RoBERTa/RoPE/mean-pooling reference) and Turn 5 (the load-bearing correction: it must live in the main ComfyUI repo's text_encoders area, not a custom node). goal_1 captures the location/existence correction (Turn 5), goal_2 captures the architecture-behavior of the encoder (Turn 4), goal_3 captures loader integration so 'in this repo' actually means selectable-and-loadable. Weights rank by user importance: the encoder behavior (goal_2, 0.40) is the engineering substance; the location correction (goal_1, 0.34) is the explicit and most-emphasized user redirect; loader wiring (goal_3, 0.26) completes it. These intentionally do not 1:1 mirror the manifest F2P weights (which split architecture into seven sub-gates of 0.06-0.20); the rubric instead groups by what the user actually asked for. All four real model patches scored 0.0 because they registered a branch in sd.py but never shipped a working encoder module — goal_0 and goal_2 jointly guard against that exact partial-credit gaming. The rubric stays implementation-agnostic: test.sh and the golden patch both accept HF-AutoModel-delegation and native re-implementation, and accept file names jina_clip.py or jina_clip_2.py, so no goal names a file, class, or function.
